coredata.dc:title: Aminolysis-assisted hydrothermal conversion of waste PET plastic to N-doped carbon dots with markedly enhanced fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Chan, Kayee
Zinchenko, Anatoly
coredata.dc:description: While the possibility of waste bottles’ PET conversion to fluorescent materials has been recently demonstrated, optimization of the conversion protocol based on rational material design may greatly enhance key characteristics of the upcycled material. In this study, conversion of PET to fluorescent nanomaterials by means of hydrothermal (HT) treatment of PET and products of PET pyrolysis, glycolysis, and aminolysis in the presence and in the absence of an oxidizing agent was systematically investigated to compare fluorescent properties of HT products. Among studied conversion routes, the direct HT conversion of PET or a combination of the HT treatment with the PET pre-treatment by either pyrolysis or glycolysis resulted in non-fluorescent of weakly fluorescent products, whereas aminolysis of PET bottle plastics followed by the HT treatment of the aminolysis products increased dramatically, up to tens to hundreds fold, the fluorescence of the products. Admixing of several percent of an oxidant, H2O2, to HT mixture further enhanced the product fluorescence yielding fluorescent nanoparticles (nitrogen-doped carbon dots, NCDs) with 25.3% conversion yield and the quantum yield of 9.1%. Based on 1H and 13C NMR, FTIR, XPS, and Raman spectroscopy data, the mechanism of NCDs formation was proposed. The N-doped carbon dots obtained by waste PET upcycling were used for detection and quantification of Cu2+ ion at ppm and Fe3+ ion at ppb concentrations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of PET-derived fluorescent materials. A-B. Photographic images of solutions after hydrothermal processing of waste PET plastic by different synthetic routes shown in Scheme 1 under daylight (A) and under 302 nm irradiation by UV lamp (B). C. Fluorescence spectra under 300 nm excitation of 1% solutions after hydrothermal processing of waste PET plastic by different synthetic routes. D. Comparison of fluorescence intensities of products prepared by different synthetic routes divided by the weight of the consumed PET plastics. The low-case letters refer to the conversion routes shown in Scheme 1, i.e., HT processing of a. PET powder in water, b. PET pyrolysis product in aqueous H2O2, c. PET glycolysis product in water, d. PET/TETA aminolysis product in water, e. PET/DETA aminolysis product in water, f. a mixture of PET and DETA in water, g. PET/DETA aminolysis product in aqueous H2O2, h. a mixture of PET and DETA in aqueous H2O2.

Characterization of PD-NCDs prepared by HT treatment of PET aminolysis product (PD) in the presence of H2O2. A. Typical TEM image of PD-NCDs purified by dialysis. B. High resolution TEM image of an individual PD-NCDs showing the lattice spacing equal 0.335 nm. C. Particle size distribution of PD-NCDs. D. Raman spectrum of PD-NCDs. E. UV spectrum of PD-NCDs solutions. F. Fluorescence spectra of PD-NCDs solutions recorded under different excitation wavelengths.

Spectroscopic characterization of PD-NCDs. A. Comparison of FTIR spectra of PET aminolysis product as prepared (PD) and PD-NCDs. B. 1H NMR spectra of PD in DMSO-d6 and HT treatment products obtained after 3 h, 6 h, and 12 h (PD-NCDs), respectively, in D2O. Signals indicated by triangle and star symbols correspond to solvent residual peaks of D2O (δ = 4.79 ppm) and DMSO-d6 (δ = 2.50 ppm), respectively, used as internal standards. C. 13C NMR spectrum of PD-NCDs in D2O.
XPS characterization of PD-NCDs. A. XPS survey spectrum of PD-NCDs. B-D. High resolution C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s spectra (D).

Metal ions sensing by PD-NCDs. A. Selectivity of 25.3 mg/L of PD-NCDs for 0.33 mM of various metal ions. White bar corresponds to the original fluorescence intensities of 25.3 mg/L solution of PD-NCDs without metal ions. I and

0
referred to the fluorescence intensities of PD-NCDs at 300 nm with and without metal ions, respectively. B. Fluorescence spectra of 42.2 mg/L of PD-NCDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ion from 0 to 166 μM under 300 nm excitation. C. Dependence of fluorescence intensity of 42.2 mg/L solution of PD-NCDs on Fe3+ concentration under 300 nm excitation. D. White bars correspond to the fluorescence intensities of PD-NCDs solutions without cations.

The color of circles behind the letters of the corresponding routes (a - h) shows actual fluorescent light emitted under 302 nm UV light excitation of the reaction products under the same conditions.

head.ce:title: Aminolysis-assisted hydrothermal conversion of waste PET plastic to N-doped carbon dots with markedly enhanced fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Environmental Studies, Nagoya University, Furo-cho, Chikusa-ku, Nagoya 464-8601, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Environmental Studies, Nagoya University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School of Environmental Studies, Nagoya University, Furo-cho, Chikusa-ku, Nagoya, 464–8601, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While the possibility of waste bottles’ PET conversion to fluorescent materials has been recently demonstrated, optimization of the conversion protocol based on rational material design may greatly enhance key characteristics of the upcycled material. In this study, conversion of PET to fluorescent nanomaterials by means of hydrothermal (HT) treatment of PET and products of PET pyrolysis, glycolysis, and aminolysis in the presence and in the absence of an oxidizing agent was systematically investigated to compare fluorescent properties of HT products. Among studied conversion routes, the direct HT conversion of PET or a combination of the HT treatment with the PET pre-treatment by either pyrolysis or glycolysis resulted in non-fluorescent of weakly fluorescent products, whereas aminolysis of PET bottle plastics followed by the HT treatment of the aminolysis products increased dramatically, up to tens to hundreds fold, the fluorescence of the products. Admixing of several percent of an oxidant, H2O2, to HT mixture further enhanced the product fluorescence yielding fluorescent nanoparticles (nitrogen-doped carbon dots, NCDs) with 25.3% conversion yield and the quantum yield of 9.1%. Based on 1H and 13C NMR, FTIR, XPS, and Raman spectroscopy data, the mechanism of NCDs formation was proposed. The N-doped carbon dots obtained by waste PET upcycling were used for detection and quantification of Cu2+ ion at ppm and Fe3+ ion at ppb concentrations.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, man-made plastics have become an indispensable part of everyday life. Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) is one of the mass-produced plastics which is widely used for packaging, containers, films and fibers [1] due to its excellent properties such as hydrophobicity, chemical inertness, high stability, and negligible permeability to CO2 [2]. Owing to its progressive accumulation in the environment, the problem of PET’s utilization has drawn considerable attention. Until now, recycling methods of PET have largely relied on the direct mechanical, thermal, or chemical processing technologies [3]. On the other hand, the number of newly proposed strategies for waste PET upcycling to functional materials has grown sharply over recent years. Waste PET was successfully utilized in construction and composite materials [4], oil spills adsorbents [5], sound isolation aerogels [6], hydrogel adsorbents for water cleaning [7], benzoxazine resins [8], paving materials [9], etc. Synthesis of carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and nanoporous carbon from waste PET plastic has been also actively developing [2,10–12].

Carbon nanoparticles (carbon dots, CDs) having typical size below 10 nm have attracted tremendous attention owing to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, high water solubility, good optical properties, and other useful characteristics [13–15]. Due to these attributes, CDs have been employed in a broad number of applications in fields of sensing [16], bioimaging [17], catalysis [18], biomedicine [19], etc. In contrast to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) prepared form toxic chemicals [20], CDs and polymeric biodots are usually synthesized from environmentally benign biomass precursors such as cellulose [21], starch [22], DNA [23], etc., using hydrothermal (HT) synthesis [24] or microwave-assisted conversion [25]. Such CDs are not only a promising alternative to conventional fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterials in traditional fields, but the utilization of waste biomass precursors for their synthesis also meets the needs of waste recycling.

Waste plastic is another promising yet relatively unexplored source of organic precursors that can be utilized for preparation of functional carbon nanomaterials. In their pioneering study, Hu et al. developed a two-step process of air oxidation of PET plastic followed by its hydrothermal treatment [26] and demonstrated an application of obtained carbon dots for sensing of ferric and pyrophosphate ions. Development of such plastic waste valorization technologies will greatly contribute to the sustainable materials cycle and circular economy. However, the reported methods of carbon dots preparation from waste plastic, to the best of our knowledge, are limited to a few recent studies [26–28] reporting the proof of concept, whereas the mechanism of CDs formation from waste plastic PET is still unclear.

In this report, we sought for the enhancement of fluorescent characteristics of PET-derived materials by chemical pre-treatment and examined various strategies of chemical transformation of waste PET bottles by means of hydrothermal synthesis coupled with thermal (pyrolysis) or chemical PET decomposition using glycolysis or aminolysis reactions. We found that highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots can be prepared directly by a hydrothermal treatment of PET aminolysis products in the presence of an oxidizing agent, H2O2. The mechanism of PET-derived fluorescent N-doped carbon dots formation and their structure were systematically studied and their application for sensing of Fe3+ and Cu2+ cations was demonstrated.

PET was obtained directly from waste soft drink bottles. Bottles were washed with ultrapure water, dried at 70 °C for 1 h in oven, and cut into chips no larger than 1 cm × 0.5 cm. Diethylenetriamine (DETA) and triethylenetetramine (TETA) were purchased from Kishida Chemical Co., Ltd. (Japan). Ethylene glycol (EG), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and zinc acetate were purchased from Fujifilm Wako Pure Chemical Co. (Japan). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and 99.9% dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO-d6) for NMR measurements were purchased from Kanto Chemical Co., Inc. (Japan). Deuterium oxide (D2O) (99.8%) for NMR measurements was purchased from Fujifilm Wako Pure Chemical Co. (Japan).

KNO3, NaCl, CaCl2·2H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O from Fujifilm Wako (Japan), Na2CO3 from Kanto Chemical (Japan), MgSO4 from Nacalai Tesque (Japan), Hg(NO3)2·H2O and AgNO3 from Sigma-Aldrich (USA), Cd(NO3)2·9H2O from Combi-Blocks (USA), MgCl2·6H2O and NaNO3 from Kishida Chemical (Japan) were used as received. Unless otherwise mentioned, ultrapure water purified by Purelab Chorus 1 Life Science apparatus was used in all experiments.
UV–vis spectrum of NCDs in ultrapure water was recorded on a V-630 Bio spectrometer (Jasco, Japan) in quartz cell with an optical path 1 cm at room temperature.

Fluorescent spectra of HT treatment product in ultrapure water were recorded on a FP-6600 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan) in 1 cm × 1 cm × 5 cm quartz cells (optical path 1 cm) at a room temperature at 275–400 nm excitation wavelengths.

Fourier transform infrared spectra of PET aminolysis products and freeze-dried NCDs were recorded on a FTIR-460 spectrometer (Jasco, Japan) at room temperature. A powder of a sample was placed between KBr thin plates and compressed to form a thin disc. Finally, the samples were scanned in a wavenumber range between 4000 cm−1 and 400 cm−1.

TEM observations were performed using a JEM-2100 Plus microscope (JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV acceleration voltage. A drop of a solution containing NCDs after dialysis was placed onto thin carbon film coated TEM grids (Alliance Biosystems, Japan). The solution was removed after 3 min of deposition with a filter paper, and the grids were dried in a dry box overnight (relative humidity < 10%) before observation.

DMSO-d6 solution (0.55 mL) of PET aminolysis products (ca. 3 mg) or D2O solution (0.55 mL) of NCDs (ca. 3 mg) were transferred into an NMR tube and 1H NMR spectra were measured on a JNM-ECA500 instrument (JEOL, Japan). Chemical shifts of 1H NMR were recorded in parts per million (ppm, δ) relative to the solvent residual peaks as internal standards (DMSO-d6: δ = 2.50 ppm, D2O: δ = 4.79 ppm). D2O solution (0.8 mL) of PET-derived NCDs (ca. 4.5 mg) and 10% CH3CN in D2O solution (10 μL) were mixed and transferred into an NMR tube and 13C NMR spectrum was measured. Chemical shifts of 13C NMR were recorded in parts per million (ppm, δ) relative to the solvent resonance as an internal standard (CH3 of CH3CN: δ = 1.47 ppm).

XPS spectra were recorded on a ESCALAB250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK), using an analyzer pass energy of 200.0 eV. Data acquisition and processing including XPS spectrum fitting were performed using the Advantage software (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK).
Raman spectrum of 100 μL NCDs solution containing 50 mM of Fe3+ to suppress NCDs’ fluorescence was recorded on an NRS-1000 spectrometer (Jasco, Japan) using a 532 nm laser as an excitation source.
Screening of various PET conversion routes having a single or combined conversion steps was carried out as illustrated in Scheme 1 and described below.

Preparation of PET powder. PET powder was prepared by dissolution-reprecipitation treatment of PET bottle chips using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as a solvent. 0.5 g of PET chips and 15 mL of DMSO were added into a 50 mL round bottom flask equipped with a stirrer and a reflux condenser and heated at 180 ± 5 °C until all the PET chips were dissolved (ca. 1.5 h). The resulted solution was chilled to ambient temperature, the precipitate of PET was collected, washed by ultrapure water, filtrated, and lyophilized.

PET pyrolysis. 2.5 g of PET was heated to 700 °C in an ARF1-1400 tube furnace (Asahi Rika, Japan) at a constant heating rate of 20 °C per min under the N2 atmosphere, kept at 700 °C for 0.5 h and chilled to ambient temperature under the N2 flow.

PET glycolysis. The PET glycolysis reaction was carried out at PET/ethylene glycol (EG) ratio of 1:3 (w/w). 1 g of PET chips, 3 mL of EG, and 0.01 g of zinc acetate as a catalyst were added into a 20 mL round bottom flask equipped with a stirrer and a reflux condenser. The glycolysis reaction was carried out at 195 ± 5 °C until all the PET chips were dissolved (ca. 6.5 h). The resulted solution was chilled to ambient temperature, the precipitate of PET glycolysis product was collected, washed by ultrapure water, filtrated, and lyophilized.

PET aminolysis. The PET aminolysis reaction was carried out at PET/diethylenetriamine (DETA) and PET/triethylenetetramine (TETA) ratios of 1:1 (w/w). 1 g of PET chips, 1 mL of DETA or TETA and 0.01 g of zinc acetate as a catalyst were added into a 20 mL round bottom flask equipped with a stirrer and a reflux condenser. The aminolysis reactions were carried out at 195 ± 5 °C until all the PET chips were dissolved (ca. 30 min). The resulted solution was chilled to ambient temperature and used without further purification.

Hydrothermal synthesis. HT synthesis was performed by dispersing a starting material (PET or a product of PET pyrolysis, glycolysis or aminolysis) either in an ultrapure water or in 4% aqueous H2O2 at constant concentration of the starting material (1% (w/v)). The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL PTFE lined HT autoclave reactor vessel HU-25 (SAN-AI Kagaku, Japan) and the HT treatment was carried out either at 250 °C in ultrapure water for 5 h or at 180 °C in 4% aqueous H2O2 for 12 h. After HT treatment, the reaction mixture was chilled to ambient temperature and a precipitate (if present) was separated by centrifugation.

The product mixture obtained after HT treatment of PET aminolysis product was purified by dialyzing against 500 mL ultrapure water using Slide-A-Lyzer dialysis cassettes (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Japan) with molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) 2000 Da two times for 2 h and one time for 6 h. The solutions of NCDs after dialysis were stored at a room temperature and used for analysis as prepared. Solid samples of NCDs purified by the dialysis were prepared by removing water solvent using a rotary evaporator and lyophilizing overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal processing of PET and PET decomposition products
ce:section.ce:para:
Various strategies to prepare fluorescent materials from polyethylene terephthalate of PET bottle have been investigated (Scheme 1 and Table S1) including the hydrothermal treatment of (i) PET powder, (ii) PET pyrolysis products, (iii) PET glycolysis products, and (iv) PET aminolysis products. Furthermore, hydrothermal treatment of the starting materials in the presence of an oxidizer, H2O2, was also performed. Preliminary screening was carried out to optimize HT treatment time and temperature as well as the concentration of H2O2 solution by utilizing PET aminolysis product (Fig. S1 ). Based on the above screening of HT treatment conditions, unless otherwise mentioned, comparison of fluorescent characteristics of the synthesized products was performed under the same conditions, i.e., either 5 h treatment at 250 °C in ultrapure water or 12 h treatment at 180 °C in 4% H2O2 solution using 1% of PET starting materials in each case. The fluorescent characteristics of the reaction products obtained via routes a-h ( Scheme 1 ,
Fig. 1 A,B) were assessed by measuring their fluorescence spectra under 300 nm excitation ( Fig. 1 C). Fluorescence intensities of the corresponding reaction products divided by actual weight of the consumed PET are compared in Fig. 1 D.

The direct HT processing of PET powder form PET bottle in water at 250 °C for 5 h ( Scheme 1 , route a) yielded crystal product with no fluorescence ( Fig. 1 , a). Further conversions of PET were performed by combining the HT treatment with either thermal or chemical pre-treatments of PET ( Scheme 1 , routes b-h). First, pyrolysis of PET was carried out at 700 °C for 0.5 h under N2 atmosphere followed by the HT treatment in the presence of oxidizing agent (H2O2) at 180 °C for 12 h ( Scheme 1 , route b). However, the two-step treatment also yielded no fluorescent product ( Fig. 1 , b). It was thus concluded that the PET and its pyrolysis product are not suitable precursors, probably, due to lack of functional groups that would promote the formation of fluorescent materials.

Next, the chemical pre-treatment of PET was performed prior to conducting HT treatment. Depolymerization reactions that are generally applied for chemical recycling of PET were utilized to produce more chemically functionalized low-molecular-weight precursors. Formation of such precursors with an enhanced reactivity was considered to facilitate the formation of fluorescent materials. Glycolysis, which is one of the most basic depolymerization reactions of PET, leads to the cleavage of ester linkages of PET in the presence of an excess of glycol and the replacement by hydroxyl terminals [1,29]. After the depolymerization of PET in the excess of ethylene glycol, water-insoluble decomposition products were dispersed in water at 1% concentration and subjected to HT treatment at 250 °C for 5 h ( Scheme 1 , route c). The product of the HT reaction was a non-fluorescent solution that contained a white precipitate ( Fig. 1 , c).

Aminolysis is another method that has been widely used for chemical depolymerization of PET to amidoamines [8,30]. PET aminolysis reactions were carried at 1:1 (w/w) ratios of PET to triamines (diethylenetriamine, DETA) or PET to tetramines (triethylenetetramine, TETA) ( Scheme 1 , routes d and e) and amidoamines insoluble in water at room temperature were obtained. Hydrothermal treatments of 1% dispersion of PET aminolysis products in water at 250 °C for 5 h yielded light-yellow solution containing no precipitate that was highly fluorescent ( Fig. 1 , d and e). Specifically, compared to other pre-treatment methods, aminolysis route enhanced the product fluorescence by tens to hundreds fold ( Fig. 1 C). Shorter treatment times, i.e., 3 h, negatively affected the fluorescent properties of the reaction product, while there was essentially no enhancement in the product fluorescence at longer times of HT treatment ( Fig. S1A ). The fluorescent product prepared from amidoamines of PET and DETA had ca. 20% higher fluorescence intensity than the product prepared from PET and TETA under the same HT conditions.

Previous studies [31,32] reported that depolymerization reactions of PET occurred during hydrothermal treatment. Therefore, an attempt to optimize the above two-step synthetic route ( Scheme 1 , route e) by carrying out a single-step conversion of PET in the presence of DETA directly in the HT reactor was undertaken ( Scheme 1 , route f). The mixture of 1% PET and DETA at 1:1 ratio in water was subjected to the HT treatment at 250 °C for 5 h. This single-step treatment also yielded the fluorescent products ( Fig. 1 , f) but with about 4 times lower fluorescence intensity. Therefore, while aminolysis of PET and the formation of fluorescent product seems to take place simultaneously during the single-pot hydrothermal treatment, such direct processing negatively affects the fluorescent characteristics of the resulted material. The above results make it clear that the fluorescent material could be prepared only when the amine was used as a reactant, i.e., either by pretreatment of PET with amine before HT processing or by the direct reaction between PET and amine during the hydrothermal reaction.

In past literature [33–35], chemical oxidation process has been commonly employed for preparation of fluorescent materials. To further enhance the fluorescent characteristics of PET-derived materials, H2O2 oxidizer was added at 4% concentration to the product of PET aminolysis by DETA or to the mixture of PET and DETA, and the hydrothermal syntheses were performed at 180 °C for 12 h. Fluorescence intensities of products prepared in the presence of H2O2 were much higher compared to HT synthesis in water ( Fig. 1 , g and h). Beside the changes in the fluorescence intensity, the wavelength of the maximum fluorescence of products prepared in the presence of H2O2 was about 430 nm in contrast to about 380 nm maximum fluorescence emission wavelength typical for HT products prepared in water ( Fig. 1 C). The use of oxidizer required milder temperature conditions of HT treatment to prevent deep decomposition of the starting material accompanied by the total loss of material’s fluorescence ( Fig. S1B ). Likewise, elevation of H2O2 concentration to 8% resulted in fluorescent products with about 2.5 times lower fluorescence intensity of the product apparently by its overoxidation and decomposition ( Fig. S1C ). Noteworthy, while the organic amines used in this study determine the excellent fluorescence characteristics of HT reaction products, HT processing of amines without PET, as well as the processing of PET without amines described above, were not efficient. For example, HT treatment of pure DETA with no added PET in 4% H2O2 solution yielded no fluorescent product ( Fig. S2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-doped carbon dots prepared by oxidative HT treatment of PET aminolysis product
ce:section.ce:para: After screening of various experimental conditions to convert PET to the fluorescent material ( Scheme 1 ), the hydrothermal treatment of products of PET aminolysis with DETA in the presence of H2O2 was selected as the optimal route ( Fig. 1 , g). The fluorescent product thus prepared was subjected to systematic characterization by TEM, NMR, FTIR, XPS, Raman, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopies. Prior characterization, the fluorescent material was dialyzed against a membrane with MWCO 2000 Da (pore size ca. 1 nm) to remove low-molecular-weight co-products.

The synthetic yield of fluorescent nanomaterial after dialytic purification was 25.3%. Typical TEM image (
Fig. 2 A) showed carbon nanoparticles of d = 12.9 ± 3.9 nm, having a broad size distribution and non-uniform shapes ( Fig. 2 C). High resolution TEM image (Fig. 2 B) revealed the presence of crystal fringes with ~0.34 nm lattice spacing corresponding to (002) planes of graphite indicating the graphitic structure of the obtained carbon nanomaterial [36,37]. The graphitic structure is characteristic for carbon dots (CDs), therefore, the obtained product will be referred hereafter as PD-NCDs. The Raman spectrum of PD-NCDs had the characteristic D and G bands at 1428 and 1616 cm−1, respectively ( Fig. 2 D) [38–40]. The G band (sp2 hybridization) in Raman spectrum was assigned to the vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, while the D band (sp3 hybridization) was attributed to the presence of structural defects [41,42]. The intensity ratio between D band and G band (
I
D
/
I
G
) is a measure of the defects present in the structure. The
I
D
/
I
G
ratio of PD-NCDs was turned out to be 0.54 and such a low
I
D
/
I
G
ratio suggests that the PD-NCDs are composed of crystalline graphitic structure with a moderate part of defects. The appearance of such defects can be ascribed to nitrogen doping [41,43] as well as to another type of surface functionalization.

The optical properties of PD-NCDs were studied using UV–vis and fluorescent spectroscopies. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of PD-NCDs showed a peak at λ = 288 nm, which was associated with the
π −
π
*
transition of aromatic CC bonds in the graphitic core of carbon dots, while another peak at 313 nm corresponded to the
n −
π
*
transition of CO groups on the surface of PD-NCDs ( Fig. 2 E) based on the assignment suggested earlier [44–46]. The fluorescence spectra of PD-NCDs ( Fig. 2 F) contained a single emission peak with the maximum fluorescence intensity at the wavelength around 435 nm. No significant changes in the position of the peak were observed by changing excitation wavelength (λex) in a range between 275 and 400 nm, yet the fluorescence intensity varied significantly, and the maximum fluorescence was measured at λex = 300 nm. The pH effect on fluorescence intensity of PD-NCDs is shown in ( Fig. S3 ). No change in PD-NCDs fluorescence intensity under alkaline condition was observed, while the fluorescence intensity of PD-NCDs decreased by ca. 40% under acidic condition presumably due to the protonation of amines and nitrogen-containing functional groups of PD-NCDs [47]. The quantum yield (QY) was measured using quinine sulfate as a reference standard and the QY of PD-NCDs was calculated to be 9.1%, which is higher than the QY of the carbon dots prepared from PET in the previous work [26].

FTIR spectra of PET aminolysis product (PD) and PD-NCDs are compared in Fig. 3 A. Relatively strong peaks of PD at 1636 and 1540 cm−1 are associated with CO stretching and N-H bending of the secondary amide group, respectively [48]. The absorption peaks of PD-NCDs at 1660 and 1613 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching of CO and CC/CN groups, respectively. However, characteristic absorption peaks of PD amide bonds almost disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of PD-NCDs indicating the decomposition of PD backbone during hydrothermal synthesis. On the other hand, appearance of a new strong peak at 1381 cm−1 characteristic for aromatic C-N stretching vibration [49] confirms the formation of a structure composed of sp2 hybridized carbons during HT synthesis and corroborates the graphitic structure of PD-NCDs revealed by TEM and Raman spectroscopy.

Additional analysis of functional groups in PD-NCDs was next carried out by proton and carbon NMR spectroscopies. Signals at 1.8–2.6 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum of PD-NCDs ( Fig. 3 B) are assigned to the sp3 C-H protons, while signals observed between 3.2 and 4.0 ppm are due to the presence of hydroxy and amino groups. The aromatic ring signals or sp2 C-H protons are detected in the 7.7–8.0 ppm region [50,51]. To get deeper insight into structural changes of HT treatment products, the intermediates prepared by 3 h and 6 h of HT treatment were also characterized ( Fig. 3 B). In the spectrum of the original PD ( Fig. 3 B), signals between 2 and 3 ppm correspond to the methylene group (-CH2-) adjacent to the amide group [7] and the integrated value of these sp3 C-H protons was found to be 38.6% of the total intensity of protons ( Table S2 ). Drastic decrease of the percentage of sp3 C-H protons after 3 h of HT treatment to 8.4% ( Table S2 ) points out to fast decomposition of PD backbone within a few hours of HT treatment. Subsequently, the ratio (γ) of aromatic (7.7–8.0 ppm) to non-aromatic protons (1.8–4.0 ppm) in the 1H NMR spectrum of PD-NCDs (γ = 2.33) was much higher than that of PD (γ = 0.40) ( Fig. 3 B and Table S2 ). This tendency substantiates the formation of polyaromatic structure of PD-NCDs abundant with sp2 C-H protons. Relatively low γ ratios of products obtained after 3 h (γ = 0.98) and 6 h (γ = 0.94) of HT treatment suggest that the polymerization and the assembly of polyaromatic structure occurs primarily at the later stages of hydrothermal treatment. Polyaromatic nature of PD-NCDs is further supported by prevailing signals of CC aromatic or sp2 hybridized carbons in the region 100–150 ppm ( Fig. 3 C) in 13C NMR spectrum. Low intensity signals of sp3 hybridized carbons (C-C/C-O) in the region 25–70 ppm and signals of carbonyl carbons in the region 170–180 ppm indicate moderate functionalization of the polyaromatic core with oxygen-containing groups. Time dependence of HT products’ fluorescence intensity ( Fig. S4 ) shows that decomposition products of PD formed at earlier stages of HT treatment are also fluorescent but longer times of HT reaction cause a significant increase in fluorescence intensity.

The XPS analysis was performed to obtain further information about the chemical composition and bonding states of atoms in PD-NCDs (
Fig. 4 ). The survey spectrum ( Fig. 4 A) clearly shows the pronounced signals of C 1 s (57.8%) at around 286.0 eV, N 1 s (13.3%) at around 400.7 eV and O 1 s (28.8%) at around 532.5 eV. High percentages of N and O in PD-NCDs confirm significant N-doping of the carbon dots and their high functionalization shown by other characterization methods. A high-resolution C 1 s spectrum ( Fig. 4 B) exhibited six carbon bonding states of C-C/CC at 285.0 eV, C-O/C-N at 286.5 eV and CO/CN at 288.5 eV [52]. The nitrogen bonding states were confirmed by a high resolution N 1 s spectrum ( Fig. 4 C) that contained the peaks of C-N/pyrrolic-N bonds at 399.9 eV, pyridinic-N bonds at 401.7 eV and
π −
π
*
satellite features of nitrogen-containing aromatic polymer at 406.9 eV [53]. The presence of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N bonds in PD-NCDs implies that N-containing functional groups are incorporated into the sp2 aromatic carbon structure via ring-opening reaction and self-polymerization [54]. Notable contribution of pyrrolic and pyridinic states explains the existence of defects in the graphitic structure of PD-NCDs concluded based on the Raman spectrum. In addition, a high resolution O 1 s spectrum ( Fig. 4 D) indicated two oxygen bonding states of CO at 531.6 eV and C-O at 532.8 eV [55] in agreement with FTIR and NMR analysis data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing by PET-derived NCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
In a vast number of past studies on carbon dots, sensing applications using CDs were given a particular focus [56]. In this study, PD-NCDs having good fluorescent characteristics were tested for their possible applications in metal ions sensing. Firstly, fluorescence intensities of 25.3 mg/L solution of PD-NCDs containing 0.33 mM of fourteen kinds of metal ions were measured under 300 nm excitation. The strongest quenching of PD-NCDs fluorescence was observed in solutions of Fe3+ (> 90%) and Cu2+ (> 70%) ions (
Fig. 5 A). A moderate quenching of PD-NCDs fluorescence was also found in solution with Hg2+ ion. On the other hand, a surprising enhancement of PD-NCDs fluorescence by ca. 20% was observed in solutions containing Al3+ ion, but the detailed analysis showed rather complex non-monotonical character of PD-NCDs spectral changes dependent on Al3+ concentration ( Fig. S5 ). Fluorescence intensities of PD-NCDs solutions containing other metal ions were only slightly higher or lower compared to the reference PD-NCDs solution in a distilled water.

After the high sensitivity of PD-NCDs for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions was revealed, further experiments were conducted to gain a more quantitative insight into the correlation between PD-NCDs fluorescence intensity and concentration of Fe3+ ion. Fluorescence intensities of 42.2 mg/L PD-NCDs solution were measured at gradually increasing concentrations of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 5 B-C, with an increase of the concentrations of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescent intensity of PD-NCDs decreased gradually until ca. 10% of the original fluorescence, suggesting that a part of PD-NCDs fluorophores was not affected by Fe3+ ions. A linear correlation between concentration of Fe3+ ion and fluorescence intensity of PD-NCDs in the range of Fe3+ concentrations between 0 and 120 μM was observed ( Fig. 5 C) with a correlation coefficient (
R
2
) of 0.993. To further explore the sensitivity of PD-NCDs for Fe3+ ion at lower Fe3+ concentrations, the fluorescence intensities of 0.843 mg/L solution of PD-NCDs at 300 nm excitation were measured in the same manner. Fig. 5 D shows that PD-NCDs preserved high sensitivity for Fe3+ ion even at lower concentrations of PD-NCDs. The linear correlation between concentration of Fe3+ ion and PD-NCDs fluorescence intensity was still held in the range 0–10 μM with a notably low detection limit around 1 μM. According to the FTIR and XPS data, the PD-NCDs contain significant number of amino groups and aromatic hydroxyl groups. The quenching of fluorescence is originated from the formation of coordination bonds between Fe3+ ions and amino/phenolic hydroxyl groups of PD-NCDs. Formation of such coordination complexes with a stable ground state alters the electronic structure of PD-NCDs and promotes the formation of non-radiative electrons that results in fluorescence quenching [57–59].

To test the applicability of PD-NCDs for detection of Fe3+ ion in the environmental samples containing abundant mono- and dications, fluorescence intensities of PD-NCDs in solutions with Fe3+ ions were measured against background cations Na+ and Ca2+ (
Fig. 6 A-B) at up to 10-fold excess compared to Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 6 A shows a very slight, several percent, recovery of Fe3+-quenched PD-NCDs fluorescence upon the increase of the Na+ concentrations and essentially no changes in PD-NCDs fluorescence upon the addition of Ca2+ ions ( Fig. 6 B). The above results show that the quantitative analysis of Fe3+ concentration using PD-NCDs can be performed even in the environmental samples of complex ionic composition. Additional analysis was performed to address the effect of potentially competing Cu2+ ion on PD-NCDs fluorescence after quenching by Fe3+ ion ( Fig. 6 C). Contrary to the insignificant effect of Na+ and Ca2+, addition of Cu2+ ions resulted in further quenching of PD-NCDs fluorescence suggesting a strong interaction of both Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions with available fluorophores on PD-NCDs and pointing out to the synergetic character of these ions’ interaction with PD-NCDs. While qualitative detection of Fe3+ ion in an analyte in the presence of Cu2+ ion can be still performed, the effect of Cu2+ should be taken into account when quantifying the concentration of Fe3+ ion using PD-NCDs as a sensing material. To study the effects of anions on the fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions by PD-NCDs, fluorescence intensities of PD-NCDs in solutions with Fe3+ ions were measured against several environmentally relevant background anions ( Fig. 6 D) added at the same concentration as Fe3+ ions. Addition of
Cl
-
,
SO
4
2 −
and
NO
3
−
ions caused only slight changes in the degree of PD-NCDs fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ (Fig. 6 D). On the contrary, co-existence of
CO
3
2 −
ions resulted in ca. 30% recovery of PD-NCDs fluorescence due to interaction between Fe3+ and
CO
3
2 −
ions ( Fig. 6 D). Above results show a potential of PD-NCDs for detection of Fe3+ ions in the presence of anions, but also point to the limitation of PD-NCDs application for quantitative detection of Fe3+ ion in complex environmental samples such as seawater with high concentrations of ions.

The analysis of various HT processing routes for preparation of fluorescent materials from waste PET revealed that combination of aminolysis pretreatment of PET with HT processing of the resulted amidoamines is the optimal method for preparation of the fluorescent product compared to alternative strategies based on HT treatment of the pristine PET from bottles or the products of PET pyrolysis or glycolysis. The presence of an oxidizer during HT treatment greatly improves the fluorescence of the product. TEM and Raman spectroscopy analyses show that the product of HT treatment of PET aminolysis products is carbon dots having the characteristic crystalline lattice of the carbon core and thus can be classified as carbon quantum dots (CQD) according to [60]. The results of NMR, FTIR, and XPS analyses clearly show that the graphitic core of PD-NCDs contains defects due to N-doping and that it is deeply functionalized by oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups.

Many examples of fluorescent materials synthesized in reactions with amines have been reported and the important role of amines promoting formation of the fluorescent product from PET comes as no surprise. For example, organic amines in a combination with polycarbonic acids is a well-known system for synthesis of highly fluorescent N-doped carbon dots [61]. Formation of bright biodots with QY up to 30% from amino acids containing amino and carboxylic groups in the same molecule was also reported [62]. The important role of amine can be related to the N-doping of CDs and functionalization of the graphitic core of CDs with nitrogen-containing groups resulting in CD’s surface passivation phenomenon, i.e., trapping of surface electrons for more efficient radiative recombination enhancing the fluorescence of CDs. N-containing carbon precursor (PET aminolysis products) may be simultaneously involved in the process of CDs formation and surface passivation resulted in the enhancement of fluorescence intensity [63]. Lack of CDs surface passivation in HT treatment of PET or products of PET pyrolysis or glycolysis, aside from possible non-optimal treatment conditions, is considered to be the main factor of marginal fluorescent characteristics of the corresponding synthetic products. The chemical structure of the amines used for PET aminolysis has only a minor effect: fluorescent products obtained using DETA had somewhat better fluorescent properties compared to TETA. Taking into account almost the same nitrogen contents in both molecules, better conformational mobility of shorter DETA molecules may contribute to a more facile polymerization and assembly into the graphitic structure of CDs.

The presence of H2O2 oxidizer in the mixture subjected to HT treatment is also critical for the formation of highly fluorescent material. The role of H2O2 in the enhancement of CD’s fluorescent properties is considered to be the oxidation of PET-derived amidoamines to impart the reacting molecules with more functional groups that can take part in polymerization and condensation reactions as well as the post-modification of CDs surface with oxygen-containing groups [64]. An increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs introduces deserve surface defects that significantly affect the energy level of CDs leading to redshift of CD’s fluorescence [65]. This tendency coincides with the redshift in the fluorescence spectra in case of HT treatment with H2O2 (Fig. 1 C).

Based on the above considerations and the structure of PD-NCDs, the formation process of PD-NCDs can be divided into the following stages.
(i)
PD undergoes depolymerization and oxidation to the small molecules containing, predominately, hydroxy, carboxy, amino and amide functional groups. Because para-substituted aromatic rings of PET cannot be directly incorporated into the graphitic core, either oxidation of aromatic side chains and oxidative ring-opening take place first and the resulted species take part in consequent reactions [54].
(ii)
The small molecules formed in stage (i) undergo polymerization, predominately by reactions between carboxyl and amino groups to form pyrrolic or pyridinic rings through the dehydration reaction [66]. Progressive polymerization links together the reacting species to form large, fused rings [67].
(iii)
The polymeric fragments with fused rings undergo final carbonization to form the N-doped graphitic core, while the amorphous surface layer exposes different functional groups [68,69]. The presence of H2O2 may also induce post-modification of the surface layer to provide its higher functionalization [67].

Highly functionalized surface of PD-NCDs offers a large number of metal ions binding sites that affect CDs fluorescence and can be used for metal ions sensing. Sensing characteristics of PD-NCDs toward metal ions are in a good agreement with previous studies reported that Fe3+ ions can strongly interact with functional groups on the surface of CDs such as -OH and -NH2, resulting in their coordination [70]. Likewise, PD-NCDs containing many oxygen- and nitrogen-based functional groups provide plenty of chelation sites for Fe3+ ions that results in the efficient quenching of PD-NCDs’ fluorescence upon PD-NCDs binding with such ions. Excellent selectivity of PD-NCDs toward Fe3+ renders PD-NCDs promising for the detection of Fe3+ ions in the environmental and biological samples.

Comparing various strategies of hydrothermal processing of PET from waste bottles to fluorescent material, HT treatment of PET aminolysis products in the presence of an oxidizer (H2O2) was shown to be the optimal conversion process yielding N-doped carbon dots with excellent fluorescent characteristics. The obtained PET-derived N-doped carbon dots can be used for a sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in aqueous solutions at ppb and ppm concentrations, respectively. The results of our study demonstrate that the hydrothermal treatment of waste plastic PET coupled with a rational chemical design of the conversion route can be a promising strategy to go beyond traditional waste PET-derived chemical feedstocks towards new functional materials with markedly enhanced properties.
Kayee Chan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Anatoly Zinchenko: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft.
